narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, like your full-time student status—which is the norm for over 90% of applicants—and your LSAT score of 37, which edges slightly above the typical 36.7 average. However, the admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' bar exam outcomes, predicted a 56% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, tipping the scales just over the threshold for rejection. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record that carried the most weight in the decision. The strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, which falls below the average of 3.21 and signaled to the model a potential challenge in sustaining the high performance needed for the bar. Next in influence, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 5—right around the average of 5.53—didn't provide the upward momentum the model favors, instead nudging the prediction further toward failure compared to students who climbed higher in their class standings. On a brighter note, your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction back toward passing by demonstrating strong analytical skills that have helped many similar students succeed. Overall, these top three elements—your undergrad grades as the biggest drag, followed by middling later-year performance, offset somewhat by your LSAT—outweighed your other strengths and set you apart from typical admitted students who tend to show consistently stronger GPAs and grade progressions. While disheartening, this isn't a final verdict on your potential as a lawyer. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to boost it further, pursuing post-baccalaureate courses or a master's to lift your GPA profile, and gathering letters from professors who can highlight your Year 3 improvements. Many students in your position have rebounded by addressing these exact gaps, so targeting them now could open doors at other programs or in a future cycle. Keep pushing—you've already shown capability in key areas.
